intro: Pear is the classic quince-dwarfing story. A pear grafted onto quince makes a small, early-bearing tree, which is why Quince A and Quince C are the usual garden pear rootstocks, and Quince C is the one you can buy in Australia. The wrinkle unique to pears is that quince is a different species, so it dwarfs the tree but is graft-incompatible with several popular varieties, which then need a compatible interstem. Where you want a big, long-lived tree, or want to sidestep the incompatibility altogether, a pear seedling or an OHxF pear-on-pear rootstock is the answer.
sources_note: Rootstock guidance on this page draws on the Royal Horticultural Society, Washington State University tree-fruit extension, the Orange Pippin pear-rootstock reference, and Australian nursery rootstock listings.

sources.name: Royal Horticultural Society: rootstocks for fruit
Washington State University Tree Fruit: rootstocks for pear
Orange Pippin: rootstocks for pear trees
Heritage Fruit Trees: Quince C rootstock (retail, Australia)
Daleys Fruit Tree Nursery: rootstocks for grafting

rootstocks.name: Quince C
rootstocks.soils: Good, moist soils; not very winter hardy, and less drought and chalk tolerant than pear
rootstocks.notes: The most dwarfing common pear rootstock and the one sold in Australia, giving the smallest, earliest-bearing tree. The trade-off is the pear-quince incompatibility: several varieties (Williams or Bartlett, Bosc, Packham) will not graft lastingly onto quince and need a compatible interstem.

rootstocks.name: Quince A
rootstocks.soils: Wide range of good soils; shares quince's lower winter hardiness
rootstocks.notes: A step up from Quince C, roughly the pear equivalent of the apple rootstock MM106: more precocious and less vigorous than a seedling, but still short of the OHxF stocks for fireblight resistance, and with the same quince-pear incompatibility on some varieties.

rootstocks.name: OHxF series (Old Home x Farmingdale)
rootstocks.vigour: Semi-vigorous to near full size, by selection
rootstocks.notes: Pear-on-pear clonal rootstocks bred from two fireblight-resistant varieties. Being Pyrus, they carry no quince incompatibility and suit all pear varieties, they are winter hardy, precocious and well anchored, and the selections run from about half seedling size (OHxF 333) up to near full size (OHxF 97).

rootstocks.name: Pear seedling
rootstocks.soils: Most soils, tolerant of chalk and dry ground; long-lived
rootstocks.notes: The worldwide standard rootstock and the choice for a big, long-lived specimen or orchard tree. A pear seedling is compatible with all European pears with no incompatibility, and copes with dry, alkaline ground better than quince, but it makes a very large tree that is slow to come into bearing.

grow_your_own.intro: Pear splits neatly. A pear seedling gives a big, fully compatible tree you can raise from seed, while quince gives a small, early-bearing one, but brings the wrinkle unique to pears: graft incompatibility with some varieties. Which you raise depends on the tree you want.

sections.heading: Quince for a small tree, but check compatibility
sections.body: Quince is a different species from pear, and that distance is thought to be part of what dwarfs the tree and brings it into bearing early. It also means some varieties (Williams or Bartlett, Bosc, Packham) will not form a lasting graft directly on quince. The fix is an interstem: a short length of a quince-compatible variety such as Doyenne du Comice is grafted between the quince rootstock and your chosen variety, so the finished tree has three parts. Quince A and Quince C are named clones, so you buy or strike them rather than raise them from a pip.

sections.heading: Pear seedling for a full-size tree
sections.body: For a big, long-lived tree with no compatibility worries, a pear seedling (Pyrus communis) is the simplest home rootstock: it is compatible with all European pear varieties and is the standard rootstock worldwide. The catch is size and patience, because a seedling makes a very large tree that is slower to come into bearing than the same variety on quince.

faqs.q: What is the most dwarfing rootstock for a pear tree?
faqs.a: Quince C is the most dwarfing common pear rootstock, keeping a trained tree to about 2.5 to 3m and bringing it into bearing early, and it is the pear rootstock sold in Australia. The catch is the pear-quince incompatibility: several varieties, including Williams (Bartlett), Bosc and Packham, will not graft lastingly onto quince and need a compatible interstem, so check your variety first. Quince A makes a slightly larger tree, about 3 to 4.5m.

faqs.q: Which pear rootstock avoids the quince incompatibility?
faqs.a: The OHxF (Old Home by Farmingdale) rootstocks and a plain pear seedling are both Pyrus, so they are compatible with all European pear varieties and skip the quince incompatibility entirely. OHxF stocks add fireblight resistance and winter hardiness and come in sizes from about half to near full seedling height, while a pear seedling makes a full-size, long-lived tree. Quince is still the pick when you want the smallest tree and your variety is compatible with it.
